description: Analytical strategies for business, marketing, and financial questions. Strategies are reframings — ways of looking at a problem that change what evidence counts and what the answer must do.
usage: Apply strategies in combination with paradigms and structures. A strategy does not produce a number on its own; it disciplines how the number is produced and defended.

strategies.name: Top-Down vs. Bottom-Up
strategies.coreReframe: Compute the same quantity by starting from an industry total and filtering down, and independently by building up from units, prices, and customers; reconcile the gap.
structuralSignals: Question involves a market size, addressable revenue, or capacity estimate
Authoritative industry data is available but granular assumptions also exist
Stakeholders distrust a single-source estimate
whatItUnlocks: A reality check: large divergence between the two methods signals a hidden assumption
A defensible range rather than a false-precision point estimate
Clear identification of which filter or driver the answer is most sensitive to

classicExamples.examples: Size U.S. tele-health market top-down from total healthcare spend vs. bottom-up from visits × price × adoption.
Estimate warehouse throughput top-down from industry benchmark vs. bottom-up from pick rate × shifts × headcount.

failureModes: Building the 'independent' bottom-up estimate from the same data source as the top-down
Reconciling the gap by arbitrary averaging instead of investigating the discrepancy
Reporting only the estimate that matches the preferred narrative

strategies.name: Triangulation
strategies.coreReframe: Estimate the same quantity using three or more methodologically distinct approaches and treat agreement (or disagreement) as evidence about the answer's reliability.
structuralSignals: Single-method estimates are viewed as unreliable
Multiple independent data sources or methods are available
Stakes justify redundant analysis
whatItUnlocks: Robustness: an answer that survives three lenses is more defensible than one produced by a single model
Diagnostic power when methods disagree — the disagreement is itself information
Reduced sensitivity to any single data source's bias

classicExamples.examples: Value a company via DCF, trading comps, and precedent transactions; report the overlap range.
Measure campaign incrementality via holdout test, media-mix model, and pre/post geo-lift.

failureModes: Methods that share the same underlying bias (all three start from the same forecast)
Averaging across methods instead of investigating why they differ
Cherry-picking the two that agree and discarding the third

strategies.name: Sensitivity Analysis
strategies.coreReframe: Instead of defending a point estimate, identify which inputs actually drive the output and by how much, then concentrate evidence and debate on those.
structuralSignals: A quantitative model with many uncertain inputs
Stakeholders challenging the model's credibility
Research budget is limited and must be prioritized
whatItUnlocks: Prioritization of which assumptions actually merit deeper research
Protection against spurious precision — shows where the answer is fragile
A communication tool that separates big levers from irrelevant dials

classicExamples.examples: DCF tornado showing that terminal-growth and WACC dominate valuation, not year-2 revenue.
NPV sensitivity on a factory project showing that utilization swamps labor cost.

failureModes: Flexing inputs over arbitrary symmetric ranges unrelated to real uncertainty
Ignoring correlations between inputs so joint effects are missed
Treating the tornado as a forecast rather than a diagnostic of the forecast

strategies.name: Benchmark Comparison
strategies.coreReframe: Interpret a number by comparing it to analogous entities or prior periods rather than evaluating it in isolation; the question becomes 'relative to what' not 'is this good'.
structuralSignals: Stakeholders ask 'is this number good' with no natural yardstick
Metric is industry-specific and intuition is miscalibrated
Decision depends on relative, not absolute, performance
whatItUnlocks: Calibration — transforms a raw number into a percentile or gap
Identification of outperformance or underperformance that demands explanation
A credible narrative for boards and investors

classicExamples.examples: Evaluate a 6% monthly churn rate against public SaaS peer medians for the same ARR band.
Compare a company's operating margin against listed peers to frame a rerating thesis.

failureModes: Benchmark set is not actually comparable (different business model, geography, scale)
Averaging across a benchmark set that contains extreme outliers
Treating the benchmark as a target rather than a reference point

strategies.name: Stakeholder Weighting
strategies.coreReframe: Make explicit whose preferences and costs count, and by how much, before scoring options — so the analysis is transparent about whom it is optimizing for.
structuralSignals: Multiple stakeholder groups with conflicting interests
A decision matrix that appears objective but hides political trade-offs
Post-decision pushback from groups that feel unrepresented
whatItUnlocks: Transparency about whose objectives drive the recommendation
Ability to run the analysis under alternative weight sets and report the sensitivity
Principled handling of customer-vs-shareholder, short-vs-long-term tensions

classicExamples.examples: Weigh customer value, shareholder returns, and channel-partner margin explicitly before recommending a price change.
Explicit weights on financial return, environmental impact, and governance in a portfolio screen.

failureModes: Weights set by whoever runs the meeting rather than by governance
Hiding weights inside scoring rubrics so stakeholders cannot audit them
Changing weights mid-analysis to preserve a preferred conclusion

strategies.name: Expected Value vs. Risk-Adjusted Framing
strategies.coreReframe: Separate the expected outcome of a decision from its distribution; two options with the same EV can differ sharply on downside, volatility, or bankruptcy risk.
structuralSignals: Outcomes are uncertain and potentially asymmetric
Downside is survival-threatening even if expected value is positive
A decision hides a lottery under a single-number summary
whatItUnlocks: Explicit treatment of downside, not just average outcome
Better alignment with investor or corporate risk tolerance
Rejection of positive-EV bets that are unsurvivable on the left tail

classicExamples.examples: Compare two projects with identical NPV but different cash-flow volatility and worst-case drawdown.
Reject a positive-EV trade because its 5% tail would breach a leverage covenant.

failureModes: Using expected value alone on decisions where the downside is catastrophic
Over-correcting for risk so that all positive-EV bets become unpalatable
Ignoring path dependency — average outcome hides a sequence that causes ruin

strategies.name: Leading vs. Lagging Indicators
strategies.coreReframe: Separate metrics that describe outcomes already realized (lagging) from metrics that predict future outcomes (leading), and manage against leading ones while reporting both.
structuralSignals: Performance discussions use results metrics that cannot be changed by next quarter
Teams are surprised by downturns their KPIs did not foreshadow
Operational actions don't clearly map to reported outcomes
whatItUnlocks: Earlier detection of problems while they can still be fixed
Clearer connection between daily operational actions and eventual P&L
A reporting cadence that matches the decision cadence

classicExamples.examples: Track pipeline velocity and qualified-opportunity creation as leading indicators of revenue.
Monitor NPS and product-usage depth as leading indicators of renewal.

failureModes: Assuming a leading indicator is causal when it is merely correlated
Swapping indicators whenever the current one sends bad news
Reporting leading indicators with such a long lag that they become lagging in practice

strategies.name: In-Sample vs. Out-of-Sample Validation
strategies.coreReframe: A model or strategy that fits historical data is not yet validated; its credibility depends on performance on data it was not built on.
structuralSignals: Backtest results look surprisingly strong
Model has many parameters relative to observations
Decision will be applied prospectively, not only retrospectively
whatItUnlocks: Detection of overfitting before capital or budget is deployed
Honest performance estimates that survive contact with new data
Discipline around hyperparameter tuning and model selection

classicExamples.examples: Split 10 years of market data into in-sample fit and out-of-sample validation; report both Sharpe ratios.
Train an MMM on 2 years, validate on a holdout quarter before applying to the coming planning cycle.

failureModes: Repeatedly 'validating' on the same holdout until it effectively becomes in-sample
Ignoring regime change that makes historical out-of-sample irrelevant to future conditions
Using walk-forward validation but still peeking at future data during feature selection

strategies.name: Counterfactual Framing
strategies.coreReframe: Ask 'what would have happened without this action' rather than 'what happened after this action'; the incremental effect is the difference.
structuralSignals: A campaign, feature, or policy is being credited with a post-launch change
Trends, seasonality, or concurrent changes could explain the result
ROI, lift, or incrementality claim needs to survive scrutiny
whatItUnlocks: True incrementality rather than coincidental overlap
Protection against confirmation bias in post-launch reviews
Shared language for distinguishing 'happened after' from 'caused by'

classicExamples.examples: Evaluate a TV campaign against a geo holdout or matched-market control to isolate lift.
Estimate the effect of a 5% price increase by comparing treated stores to similar untreated stores.

failureModes: Assuming 'no intervention' equals the pre-period — ignoring the counterfactual trend
Selecting a control group that is systematically different from the treated group
Declaring victory on correlational evidence when a clean counterfactual was feasible

strategies.name: Primary vs. Secondary Sources
strategies.coreReframe: Distinguish evidence collected or computed directly (filings, transactions, surveys you ran) from evidence reported by others; weight and document them differently.
structuralSignals: Conclusion rests on market-research summaries or press coverage
Stakeholders will scrutinize the evidence provenance
Downstream claims will be quoted or regulated
whatItUnlocks: Higher-confidence claims that can be defended under audit or due diligence
Identification of facts that are actually just repeated assertions
Clearer scoping of what further research is required before deciding

classicExamples.examples: Ground an investment thesis in 10-K filings and management transcripts rather than sell-side summaries.
Commission a buyer survey rather than relying on a consultancy's published trend report.

failureModes: Treating a widely-cited secondary figure as primary because it is ubiquitous
Under-weighting a well-designed secondary source just to appear rigorous
Failing to document provenance so later readers cannot assess evidence quality

crossStrategyInteractionGuide.whatItProduces: A multi-method market estimate where both directions and an adjacent-analog lens must agree to treat the range as credible.
A risk-aware recommendation that reports both which inputs matter most and how the downside distribution behaves under plausible stress.
A defensible incrementality claim validated on data not used to build the attribution model.
A positioning story that is calibrated against peers and transparent about whose objectives drive the action.
An operating cadence that spots problems early and distinguishes self-caused changes from market drift.
A claim that survives scrutiny because independent methods each rest on documented primary evidence.
